observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor advise the Municipal Accountant
and Treasurer to continue its faithful compliance with the withholding and full
remittance of the taxes withheld within the prescribed period.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor advise the MAO and Municipal
Treasurer’s Office (MTO) to consistently maintain their good practice of religiously
observing the prescribed deadlines on the withholding of premiums/deductions and
the respective remittance thereof to the GSIS in pursuance of the GSIS Act of 1997.

We recommended that the Management continue to adopt the good practice
of promptly remitting premiums, loan amortizations, and other contributions to the
HDMF in compliance with the provisions of R.A. No. 9679.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the MAO and MTO
continue to be compliant with regulations on withholding and remittance of monthly
PhilHealth contributions within the prescribed period.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a) require the Municipal Accountant to (i) draw an adjusting entry to correct the
error in the setup of the SET Receivable; and (ii) secure the RPTAR/Taxpayer’s
Index Card from the Municipal Treasurer in order to come up with the basis for
accurate and reliable amounts of RPT/SET Receivables; and
b) instruct the Municipal Treasurer to (i) intensify collections by undertaking
effective revenue collection strategies such as periodic sending of the notice of
delinquencies and tax bills by personal or mail service and coordination with
barangay officials and national and local offices that have access to the addresses
of delinquent taxpayers; (ii) avail of all legal and administrative remedies allowed
under R.A. No. 7160; and (iii) submit the RPTAR/certified list of taxpayers and
the amount of taxes due and submit a copy of the same to the Municipal
Accountant; and (iv) maintain close coordination with the Municipal Assessor as
regards the newly assessed real properties.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. OIC-GSO and concerned Offices to (i) prepare and furnish the MAO on a
monthly basis the copies of SSMI supported with RIS and/or proof of
distribution of goods and supplies; and (ii) reconcile the RPCI with Inventory
accounts in GL; and
b. Municipal Accountant to (i) conduct a thorough review of the Inventory
accounts and tally with the Offices concerned; (ii) derecognize in the books
the items which were already consumed and/or distributed to end-users in a
timely manner; (iii) and record all procurement of inventory supplies in the
books using the Perpetual Inventory System.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Accountant to (i) draw JEVs taking up the costs of the ongoing infrastructure projects
based on actual accomplishments; and (ii) disclose relevant information in the Notes
to FS about the status, progress, and significance of the land titling process for the
reclaimed land.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant trace, analyze, and reconcile
the Due from Other Funds and Due to Other Funds accounts and make the necessary
adjustments to reflect the balances of the affected accounts and furnish the Audit
Team with the corrections made.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. promptly implement all priority development projects for the benefit of its
constituents; and
b. direct the Municipal Engineer to coordinate with the contractors to comply with
the required posting of information and publicity on
programs/projects/activities of government agencies and to inform the audit
team within ten (10) days after the award of the project or before the start of the
program/project/activity that the appropriate project signboards and/or public
notices are already posted at the project site as stated in COA Circular No. 2013-
004 dated January 30, 2013.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. HCWs and Non-HCWs concerned to provide valid proofs that they physically
reported for work and have provided direct health/support care as well as the
actual and detailed accomplishment reports relative thereto; and
b. MHO or concerned supervisor, being the head of the health facility, to ensure
that Accomplishment Reports involved COVID-19 response in line with the
prevention, detection, isolation, treatment, rehabilitation and vaccination
strategy.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor in coordination with the HRMO
(a) prepare and issue policy guidelines to address the issues on the necessity and
propriety of hiring casuals and job orders in order to limit expenditures to
programs/projects where their services are actually needed; (b) hire JO personnel
based on necessity and skills/competencies specifically required in the local projects;
(c) avoid the hiring of JOs whose functions overlap or duplicate those of the regular
employees of the Municipality; and (d) re-evaluate the personnel complement to fill-
up the vacancies.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
(a) direct the LDRRM Officer to (i) include in the preparation of the
MDRRMFIP/AIP the list of programs, projects, and activities to be
undertaken out of the unexpended balance of the prior year LDRRMF to
rationalize the use of the Special Trust Fund; (ii) strive for the timely and full
implementation of LDRRM-related PPAs to achieve the goals and objective of
the fund and the desired benefits; and (iii) secure the necessary documentation
for the donated generator set; and
(b) instruct the Municipal Accountant to draw adjusting journal entries to correct
the erroneous transfer of unexpended CO appropriations to the Trust
Liabilities -DRRM account and the transfer of unexpended balance from CY
2022 of LDRRMF appropriation to the Trust Liabilities - DRRM account.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer including all Accountable Officers to (a)
immediately transmit to the Audit Team the unsubmitted accounts and financial
reports; and (b) strictly observe the timeframes on the submission of accounts and
financial reports pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-006.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a) instruct the MPDC to facilitate the gathering of data/information needed for
the creation of an updated and well-established GAD database to have an
accurate and reliable basis in the planning and programming of PPAs as well
as in the policy formulation in order to properly address the existing and
emerging GAD issues and concerns; and
b) direct the GFPS and GAD key personnel to (i) prioritize measures on the
development and passage of a GAD Code to provide the legal backbone in the
proper implementation of GAD and (ii) comply with the provisions of the
aforementioned Circular on the submission of GAD Accomplishment Report
to facilitate the audit of the utilization of GAD budget and the efficiency and
effectiveness of interventions in addressing gender issues.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
(a) continue the enforcement in the settlement of the disallowance of
₱3,109,402.85 in accordance with the payment schedule;
(b) take necessary actions to expedite the collection of the unsettled
disallowances from KASAMA Inc.; and
(c) instruct the Municipal Accountant to submit regularly a duly certified
statement of settlement of disallowances to the Audit Team for
reconciliation of the unsettled balances per books and per SASDC.

observations.reason: The MDRRM Council substantially utilized LDRRM-related PPAs with an overall utilization of 79.93%. The Municipal Treasurer opened a separate depository account for the unexpended balance of the LDRRMF under SB Resolution No. 2022-44 dated June 08, 2022. Management failed again to include in the preparation of MDRRMFIP/AIP the PPAs for the entire unexpended balance of prior year. This finding is reiterated in Observation No. 12 of the CY 2022 AAR.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that
Management undertake the
following courses of action:
a. direct the GFPS to fully
implement the GAD
PPAs and monitor their
implementation to ensure
that optimum benefits are
derived by its constituents;
b. direct the GFPS and
GAD key personnel to
(i) undergo capacity
building programs and
training on gender and
development to enhance
their knowledge and
build their competencies
on GAD-related laws and
international commitments,
gender mainstreaming,
gender analysis, gender-
responsive planning and
budgeting, and GAD-
related tools, in this
important area.
(ii) facilitate the
establishment of the
GAD M&E system to help
and assist the concerned
officials in the proper
monitoring of the
implementation of GAD
policies and PPAs and the
evaluation of the
effectiveness of the
same; and
(iii) prioritize measures
on the development and
passage of a GAD Code
to provide the legal
backbone in the proper
implementation of GAD;
c. instruct the MPDC to
facilitate the gathering of
data/information needed
for the creation of a well-
established GAD database
to have an accurate and
reliable basis in the
planning and programming
of PPAs as well as in the
policy formulation in order
to properly address the
existing and emerging GAD
issues and concerns.
observations.reason: The GFPS has fully implemented the GAD PPAs and participated in training related to GAD. A series of meetings were conducted to facilitate the approval process of the GAD Code. The submitted GAD Database revealed that the Municipality has established the GAD Database but has not effectively updated the sex-disaggregated data as required. The GAD M&E system was not yet established.

ensure that any additional compensation (e.g. cash incentives and honoraria) granted to various organizations/units and qualified officials and employees of NGAs detailed in the Municipality is duly supported by a Sangguniang Bayan (SB) Resolution authorizing such grant and specifying the rates thereof;

adhere strictly to the provisions of DBM Compensation Policy Guidelines No. 98-1 dated March 23, 1988 to avoid the incurrence of irregular expenditures.

observations.reason: Management designated POs/CSOs as traffic personnel; however, communica-tion equipment, supplies and facilities were not provided due to limited funds.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and management agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant to (i) take up the financial assistance from various NGAs as receipts under the Trust Fund books in accordance with existing accounting policy; and (ii) record the procurement of assets and expenditures incurred under the appropriate Assets/Expenses accounts and adjust Due to NGAs correspondingly.
Management committed to comply with the audit recommendations.

We recommended and management agreed to instruct the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to (a) facilitate the transfer of unexpended LDRRMF for CY 2017 amounting to ₱3,897,239.60 to the Trust Fund account to support disaster risk reduction and management activities of the Municipality; and (b) effect the corresponding journal entries to take up the transfer from the General Fund to the Special Trust Fund.
Management agreed to implement the audit recommendations.

We recommended and management agreed to require (a) the Municipal Treasurer to inform the payees about the stale checks, and whenever found existing but due for replacement, a new check may be issued upon submission of the stale check to the Municipal Treasurer; and (b) the Municipal Accountant to draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to adjust the stale checks in the books of accounts; and strictly monitor outstanding checks at year-end for a fair presentation of accounts in the financial statements.
Adjustment of stale checks was already made on January 2018 in the books of accounts. Management agreed to implement the other audit recommendations.

We recommended that management (a) direct the Municipal Treasurer, Municipal Accountant, and Municipal Budget Officer to immediately transmit the unsubmitted accounts and financial reports; (b) strictly adhere to the timeframes on the rendition of accounts and preparation/submittal of financial reports pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009; and (c) impose appropriate sanctions i.e. withholding of salaries, against officials/employees who continually neglected to abide by the rules and regulations of Section 122 of P.D. No. 1445 and other related issuances.
During the exit conference, management commented that the delay in the submission of accounts and financial reports was due to the limited manpower resources of the Accounting Office. The two staff resigned while the other one transferred to the Treasury Office. Nonetheless, management committed to comply with the audit recommendations.

We recommended that management continually comply with the provisions of the Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR) of the National Health Insurance Act of 2013 on the proper deduction of PhilHealth contributions of its personnel and the remittance thereof within the prescribed period.

We recommended that management advice the:
Local School Board to (i) take earnest efforts to fully implement all the identified programs, projects and activities for SEF consistent with the guidelines on the utilization thereof to ensure the efficient and effective use of the Fund; and (ii) prepare an Annual Procurement Plan for all the repairs of school buildings and facilities to support procurement of project materials and/or services as stated in the SEF budget; and
Municipal Accountant to (i) perform a diligent review of all disbursement claims and supporting documents before any payments are made to make sure that the same are legal, proper and valid; and (ii) immediately transmit the unsubmitted disbursement vouchers and supporting documents for SEF transactions for the period November to December 2017.
Management agreed to coordinate with DepEd in monitoring the programs and projects under SEF to ensure that all planned programs for the year will be implemented. Management likewise committed to comply with the other recommendations.

We reiterated our previous audit recommendations that management (a) institutionalize a working and functional GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) or a similar GAD mechanism to catalyze and accelerate gender mainstreaming within the Municipality; (b) submit GAD Accomplishment and Utilization Report; (c) establish GAD Database; and (d) prioritize measures on the development and passage of a GAD Code as prescribed by the GAD law and existing regulations.
During the exit conference, the Municipal Budget Officer commented that the GAD Code was already drafted and forwarded to the Sangguniang Bayan. Management agreed to implement the other audit recommendations.

We reiterated our prior years’ audit recommendations that management (i) ensure the full implementation of all the priority projects targeted for the year for the benefit of the Municipality and the constituents in general; and (ii) refrain from charging non-investment and non-capital expenditures to the 20% Development Fund to attain the real developmental intent of the Fund.
During the exit conference, management recounted that the charging of payment of the wages for the maintenance and manning of Cop’s center/premises was already stopped in May 2017. Management committed to comply with the other audit recommendations of the Audit Team.

We recommended that management:
Strive to implement all projects, programs and activities identified under the Bottom-up Budgeting (BuB) and cope with the timetable to ensure the full enjoyment of benefits that could be derived therefrom by the constituents of the Municipality;
Cause the proper liquidation of the fund transfers and return all excess funds to the source agencies (SAs) or National Treasury, as the case may be; and
Require the Municipal Accountant to submit immediately the unsubmitted disbursements and scrutinize the completeness of supporting documents of claims before releasing the same for payment and prior to the recording of the transactions in the books of accounts.
Management committed compliance with the audit recommendations of the Audit Team.

We recommended that management require the accountable officers to settle their outstanding audit disallowance through compliance with the requirements, or payment/restitution or by any of the modes of extinguishment of obligation provided by law.

We recommended that management:
instruct the Municipal Treasurer to (i) exert effort to intensify the collections by undertaking effective revenue collection strategies such as periodic sending of notice of delinquency and tax bill by personal or mail service and coordination with barangay officials and national and local offices that have access to the addresses of delinquent taxpayers; and (ii) avail of all legal and administrative remedies allowed under R.A. No. 7160;
enforce the terms and conditions stipulated in the contract of lease relative to the period and mode of payment for rental of market stalls, imposition of surcharge/s for late and non-payment of lease to encourage space holders to promptly settle their accounts and avoid possible cancellation of their contract; and
amend and include provisions on the Contract of Lease that will clearly specify the application of advance monthly rental for unpaid rent, and the corresponding penalties and legal actions to be imposed for violating any provisions of the Lease Contract.
The revision of the Municipal Revenue Code and Market Code is ongoing to address the deficiencies noted above. Management also agreed to comply with the other audit recommendations.

refrain from using collections for advance payment of expenditure and unapproved disbursement for efficient and effective financial operation; and 

assess the necessity of maintaining the present amount of the established PCF and to possibly increase to the average level of actual requirement per month in order to avoid use of collection for payment of petty operating expenses.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the management direct the Municipal Treasurer, Municipal Accountant, and Municipal Budget Officer to immediately transmit the unsubmitted accounts and financial reports for December 2016 and, henceforth, strictly adhere to the timeframes on the rendition of accounts and preparation/submittal of financial reports pursuant to COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009.

We recommended that the management undertake the following measures:
Require the Municipal Treasurer to strictly adhere to the regulations requiring the deposit of collections daily and intact;
Evaluate controls over cash handling to ensure that government funds are properly accounted for and adequately safeguarded; and
Comply strictly with the DOF regulation and secure necessary authority therefrom should the Municipality retain its deposits with banks other than Authorized Government Depository Banks (AGDB).

We recommended that the management advice the:
Local School Board and the Municipal Budget Officer to observe the proper utilization of SEF and ensure that plans and programs for projects and activities are properly approved prior to implementation to avoid delays; and
Municipal Accountant to perform a diligent review of all disbursement claims and supporting documents before any payments are made to make sure that the same are legal, proper and valid; otherwise, the Audit Team shall be constrained to suspend/disallow in audit the deficient claims.

We reiterated our previous recommendation that the management (a) institutionalize a working and functional GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) or a similar GAD mechanism to catalyze and accelerate gender mainstreaming within the Municipality; (b) establish GAD Database; (c) submit GAD Accomplishment and Utilization Report; and (d) prioritize measures on the development and passage of GAD Code as prescribed by the GAD law and existing regulations.

We reiterated our previous year’s audit recommendations for the management to undertake the following remedial measures and courses of actions:
Require the MPDC/MBO to provide clear and explicit information on the amount of appropriations approved for Peace and Order Program (POP) under the Annual Budget to support the allocation of funds for confidential expenses of the Municipality;
Submit, thru the MBO, an excerpt or portion of the approved Annual Budget containing the programs/projects/activities to facilitate cross-referencing with the Statement of Itemized POP prepared by the MPDC/MBO; and
Advice the Municipal Accountant to (1) strictly comply with the rules and regulations provided under Joint Circular No. 2015-01 specifically on the required supporting documents in the grant of cash advance for confidential expenses; (2) examine the completeness and appropriateness of the supporting documents attached to the cash advance vouchers to avoid unnecessary delay in the grant/liquidation thereof; and (3) secure the cash advance voucher and supporting documents in a sealed envelope when submitting the same to the COA Audit Team Leader for post-audit in order to protect the confidentiality of the transaction.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the various appointive officials to discontinue the payment of membership dues to leagues/associations and the charging of meals, snacks and other contributions in favor of such leagues/associations; otherwise, succeeding payments will be disallowed in audit.

We recommended that the management:
Strive to implement all projects, programs and activities identified under Bottom-up Budgeting (BuB) to (a) ensure the full enjoyment of benefits that could be derived therefrom by the constituents of the Municipality; and (b) enhance the performance efficiency of the Municipal Government so that it could qualify for similar grants in the ensuing years; and
Instruct the Municipal Accountant to (i) take up the financial assistance from various NGAs as receipts under the Trust Fund books in accordance with existing accounting policy; (ii) record the procurement of assets and expenditures incurred under the appropriate Assets/Expenses accounts; and (iii) draw a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) taking up the following adjusting entries; viz:

We recommended that the management ensure that all its insurable properties are adequately covered by insurance with the GSIS to obtain maximum protection of the interest of the government and contribute to revenue collection efforts of the National Government as well.

We recommended that the:
Members of the SB (i) consider providing effective measures to implement the municipal ordinances on the regulation of the operations of three-wheeled public conveyance to prevent undesirable outcomes as a result of unregulated proliferation of tricycles along the municipal highways, which may pose hazards and imminent danger to pedestrians and motorists’ lives and properties; and (ii) review and amend the existing local ordinances, particularly the provisions pertaining to the fines/penalties imposed to the traffic violators, to adopt with the current changes and existing environment;
Municipal Treasurer consider maintaining records of apprehended traffic violators to monitor the number of times the said violators were caught and strictly enforce the fines/penalties imposed for violating traffic rules under the Municipal Ordinance Nos. 16, s. 2000 and 2011-02; and
Municipal Human Resource Management Officer and the Property Officer consider the necessity of hiring deputized traffic personnel to man critical or accident-prone areas and provide the necessary communication equipment, supplies and facilities for better information dissemination.

We recommended that the management require the accountable officers to settle their outstanding audit suspensions and/or disallowance through compliance with the requirements, or payments/restitution or by any of the modes of extinguishment of obligation provided by law, respectively; and employ appropriate measures and take necessary actions in order to avoid incurrence of audit disallowances in the future.

We recommended that management:

direct the Accountant to (i) prepare a Journal Entry Voucher to reclassify the erroneous recording of equipment to its proper account, including the recognition of depreciation charges thereon and (ii) prepare a certified monthly Report on the Utilization of DRRMF and submit to COA on or before the 15th day after the end of each month; and

stop the practice of charging to the LDRRMF the wages of admin-aide whose functions are not related to pre-disaster preparedness programs.

For CY 2016, other disaster preparedness and mitigation projects remained unimplemen-ted due to poor monitoring of the program implementa-tion of the management.




The Report on the Utilization of DRRMF was still not prepared and submitted to COA on or before the 15th day after the end of each month owing to numerous workloads done by the Accounting Office.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer, Municipal Accountant and Municipal Budget Officer submit the accounts and reports for the month of December and adhere strictly with the timeframes on the submission of accounts and financial reports pursuant to Section 122 of P.D. No. 1445, COA Circular No. 2009-006 and 95-006.

Local School Board and Municipal Budget Officer (1) observe the prescribed usage of SEF; and (2) provide explanation on charging made to SEF for the stated non-priority expenditures.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management (a) institutionalize a working and functional GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) or a similar GAD mechanism to catalyze and accelerate gender mainstreaming within the Municipality; (b) encourage and direct the GFPS Committee and Technical Working Group to perform its mandated tasks; (c) cause the preparation of a comprehensive GAD Plan; and (d) prioritize measures on the development and passage of GAD Code as prescribed by the GAD law and existing regulations.

Management unofficially created a GAD committee headed by the MSWDO; however, they did not fully perform their functions due to lack of familiarity with the regulations regarding GAD.




The passing of the GAD Code was not given priority due to the absence of functional GAD Focal Point System who will lead the preparation of the Code.

observations.recommendation:

Plan further on how to achieve the ideal and realistic personnel complement of the municipality and have a rationalization plan instead of resorting to Job Order Scheme and consider hiring personnel for unfilled permanent position to resolve the problem on lack of personnel;

instruct the Municipal Accountant to properly classify and record job order and service contract expenses to the appropriate expense accounts; and

direct the Budget Officer to prepare the budget in accordance with actual program implementation and utilization to present a reliable expenditure program.

Poor planning and monitoring of program implementa-tion.

enforce the terms and conditions stipulated in the contract of lease relative to the period and mode of payment for rental of market stalls, imposition of surcharge/s for late and non-payment to encourage space holders to promptly settle their accounts and avoid possible cancellation of their contract.
